content.body: Last lesson you saw that a smooth curve turns around exactly where its tangent goes flat — where f'(x) = 0. But a flat tangent sits at the top of every hill and the bottom of every valley alike, so f'(x) = 0 on its own can't tell a maximum from a minimum.

The tell is what the slope does on each side. A maximum is where the curve stops climbing and starts falling: f goes from increasing to decreasing, so the slope flips from positive to negative. A minimum is the mirror image — the curve stops falling and starts rising, so the slope flips from negative to positive.
Here's a hilltop: f(x) = −x² + 4x. Drag x from left to right across the peak at x = 2 and watch the tangent slope. How does the slope change as you pass the top?

options: It stays positive the whole time
It stays zero the whole time

sandbox.caption: Drag the point across this practice hill — the tangent slope eases to 0 right at the crest, the one place where the slope reverses sign as you pass through.

solution.body: The slope is $f'(x) = -2x + 4$. Sample it on each side of the peak at $x = 2$:
The slope flips from positive to negative — the first option. Rising then falling marks a maximum.

phases.text: Left of the peak the tangent slopes uphill: $f'(x) > 0$.
At $x = 2$ the slope passes through $0$ at the top.
Past the peak the tangent slopes downhill: $f'(x) < 0$ — the slope flips from positive to negative.

feedback.correct: Right — left of the peak the slope is positive (f rising), and right of it the slope is negative (f falling). Increasing then decreasing makes x = 2 a maximum.
feedback.incorrect: Watch the sign of the tangent slope just before and just after x = 2.
feedback.hint: f'(x) = −2x + 4: positive for x < 2, negative for x > 2 — it flips from + to −.

content.body: Now a valley: f(x) = x² − 2x. Drag x across the lowest point at x = 1 and watch the tangent slope flip. What happens to the slope as you pass the bottom?

options: It stays negative the whole time
It stays zero the whole time

sandbox.caption: Drag the point over this hill and watch the readout: the tangent slope passes through 0 at the very top, flipping sign as you cross the turn.

solution.body: The slope is $f'(x) = 2x - 2$. Sample it on each side of the bottom at $x = 1$:
The slope flips from negative to positive — the first option. Falling then rising marks a minimum.

phases.text: Approaching the bottom the tangent slopes downhill: $f'(x) < 0$.
At $x = 1$ the slope passes through $0$ at the lowest point.
Past the bottom the tangent slopes uphill: $f'(x) > 0$ — the slope flips from negative to positive.

feedback.correct: Exactly — the slope is negative as the curve falls toward the bottom, then positive as it rises away. Decreasing then increasing makes x = 1 a minimum.
feedback.incorrect: Check the tangent slope just left and just right of x = 1.
feedback.hint: f'(x) = 2x − 2: negative for x < 1, positive for x > 1 — it flips from − to +.

content.body: Generalize the pattern. If the slope f'(x) changes from positive to negative as x increases through a point, what is the graph doing there?

options: Rising the whole way — no turning point
Falling the whole way — no turning point

sandbox.caption: Drag the point through this valley — the tangent flattens to slope 0 at the lowest point, the single spot where the slope switches sign.

solution.body: Translate the slope signs into motion: positive slope means rising, negative slope means falling.
If $f'$ goes $+ \to -$ as x increases, f rises then falls — the curve tops out at a maximum (the first option).

phases.text: Positive slope means rising; negative slope means falling.
If $f'$ runs $+ \to -$ as x increases, the curve rises then falls — a maximum.

feedback.correct: Positive-to-negative slope means f increases then decreases, so the curve peaks — a maximum.
feedback.incorrect: Positive slope means rising; negative slope means falling. Work out the order those happen in as x increases through the point.
feedback.hint: + slope = rising, − slope = falling. Rising then falling makes a peak.

content.body: This is the first-derivative test: to classify a point where f'(x) = 0, look at the sign of f' on each side. A flip from + to − is a maximum (up then down); a flip from − to + is a minimum (down then up).
A single curve can hold both. Next is f(x) = x³ − 3x, which climbs to a peak, dips into a valley, then climbs again — a maximum and a minimum on the same graph.
On f(x) = x³ − 3x, predict the local maximum: drag the dot to the peak where the curve stops climbing and starts falling, then lock it in to reveal the true tangent there.

interaction.goalLabel: Drag the dot to the local maximum

sandbox.caption: Drag the point up this gentle hill — the tangent slope fades to 0 exactly at the crest, then runs negative on the way down.

solution.body: The slope runs $+ \to -$ at $x = -1$ (rising then falling), so the local maximum is at $x = -1$. Drag the dot there.

phases.text: The slope runs $+ \to -$ at $x = -1$ (rising then falling) — the local maximum.

feedback.correct: Nailed it — at x = −1 the curve turns from rising to falling, so the tangent is flat (f'(−1) = 0): a local maximum.
feedback.incorrect: Aim for the hilltop, where the curve switches from going up to going down, then lock it in.
feedback.hint: f'(x) = 3x² − 3 = 0 at x = ±1; the peak (rising → falling) is the one on the left, x = −1.

content.body: On the same curve, tap the local minimum — the valley where the curve stops falling and starts rising.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point on this hill — the tangent goes flat right at the top, where the slope passes through 0. A turning point is exactly where the slope is 0.

solution.body: At $x = 1$ the slope runs $- \to +$ (falling then rising), so this is the local minimum — the valley on the right. Tap $x = 1$.

phases.text: At $x = 1$ the slope runs $- \to +$ (falling then rising) — the local minimum.

feedback.correct: Right — at x = 1 the curve turns from falling to rising, so f'(1) = 0 and it's a local minimum (the valley).
feedback.incorrect: Look for the low point, where the curve switches from going down to going up.
feedback.hint: Of the two turning points x = ±1, the valley (falling → rising) is the one on the right, x = 1.

content.body: Try it with algebra. For f(x) = x³ − 3x², f'(x) = 3x² − 6x. Plug in x = 0, x = 1, and x = 2, then drag a label onto each to classify it as a maximum, a minimum, or neither.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point along this practice curve — the tangent goes flat (slope 0) at each turning point: over the hump and again down in the dip.

solution.body: Factor the derivative to find the critical points:
Test the sign of $f'$ around each: at $x = 0$ it runs $+ \to -$ (a maximum); at $x = 2$ it runs $- \to +$ (a minimum). Since $f'(1) = -3 \ne 0$, $x = 1$ isn't a critical point — neither.

phases.text: Factor the derivative: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x = 3x(x - 2) = 0$, so the critical points are $x = 0$ and $x = 2$.
Sign of $f'$ at each: $x = 0$ runs $+ \to -$ (a maximum); $x = 2$ runs $- \to +$ (a minimum).

feedback.correct: Right — f'(x) = 3x(x − 2) is zero only at x = 0 and x = 2. At x = 0 the slope runs + → − (rising then falling), a maximum; at x = 2 it runs − → + (falling then rising), a minimum. At x = 1, f'(1) = −3 ≠ 0, so the curve is just falling there — neither.
feedback.incorrect: First find where f'(x) = 3x(x − 2) equals 0 — only those x are candidates. Then check the sign of f' just left and right of each. A point where f' isn't 0 (like x = 1) can't be a max or min.
feedback.hint: 3x(x − 2) = 0 only at x = 0 and x = 2. + → − is a max, − → + is a min; since f'(1) = −3 ≠ 0, x = 1 is neither.

content.body: You can now locate and classify turning points: solve f'(x) = 0 to find the candidates, then read the sign change of f' to tell a peak from a valley.
That wraps up reading a graph with derivatives. Next, you'll switch from the slope of a curve to the area beneath it — the integral.
Below is the graph of the DERIVATIVE $f'(x)$ of $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$. Tap the $x$ where $f$ has a local MAXIMUM.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point over this hill — the tangent slope passes through 0 at the peak, where the curve stops rising and starts falling.

feedback.correct: Yes — $f'$ is positive for $x < -1$ and negative just after, so it crosses zero downward at $x = -1$. A $+ \to -$ crossing means $f$ rises then falls: a local maximum.
feedback.incorrect: This curve is $f'$, not $f$. A maximum of $f$ sits where $f'$ crosses zero from positive (f rising) to negative (f falling). Find that downward crossing.
feedback.hint: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 0$ at $x = \pm 1$. At $x = -1$, $f'$ goes $+ \to -$ (a max); at $x = 1$ it goes $- \to +$ (a min).

content.body: Below is the same DERIVATIVE $f'(x)$ of $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$. Tap the $x$ where $f$ has a local MINIMUM.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point down into this valley — the tangent slope hits 0 at the bottom, where the curve stops falling and starts rising.

feedback.correct: Right — at $x = 1$, $f'$ rises through zero from negative to positive. A $- \to +$ crossing means $f$ falls then rises: a local minimum.
feedback.incorrect: Read $f'$: a minimum of $f$ is where $f'$ crosses zero from negative (f falling) to positive (f rising). The downward crossing at $x = -1$ is the maximum instead.
feedback.hint: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 0$ at $x = \pm 1$. The $- \to +$ crossing is at $x = 1$ (a min); the $+ \to -$ crossing at $x = -1$ is the max.

content.body: f(x) = x³ − 3x has critical points at x = −1 and x = 1. Label each interval as increasing or decreasing.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point across this hill — the tangent slope reads positive on the way up, 0 at the crest, and negative on the way down.

feedback.correct: Exactly — f'(x) = 3x² − 3 is positive outside (−1, 1) and negative inside, so f climbs, dips, then climbs again.
feedback.incorrect: Plug a test x from each interval into f'(x) = 3x² − 3 and read off its sign.
feedback.hint: f' < 0 means decreasing, f' > 0 means increasing. Test x = −2, x = 0, and x = 2.

content.body: Classify each critical point of f(x) = x³ − 3x as a maximum, a minimum, or neither.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point through this valley — the tangent slope reads negative as the curve falls, 0 at the bottom, then positive as it climbs out.

feedback.correct: Right — f' = 3x² − 3 is zero at x = ±1: x = −1 runs + → − (a maximum) and x = 1 runs − → + (a minimum). At x = 0, f' = −3 ≠ 0, so it's neither.
feedback.incorrect: Only x = ±1 are critical (f' = 0). Check the sign change of f' at each; x = 0 isn't a critical point at all.
feedback.hint: + → − is a max, − → + is a min. f'(0) = −3 ≠ 0, so x = 0 is neither.

content.body: Each function below has one critical point at x = −b/2a. Match every function to that x-value.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point over this hill — the tangent slope drops to 0 at the single high point, the curve's one turning point.

feedback.correct: Nice — setting f' = 0 gives x = −b/2a each time: 2, −3, 1, and 4.
feedback.incorrect: For ax² + bx, the critical point sits at x = −b/2a. Read off a and b for each.

content.body: Below is the graph of the DERIVATIVE $f'(x) = 2x - 6$ of $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 5$. Tap the $x$ where $f$ has its MINIMUM.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point along this curve — at each turning point the tangent goes flat (slope 0): once over the hump and once down in the dip.

feedback.correct: Right — $f'(x) = 2x - 6$ is negative before $x = 3$ and positive after, crossing zero upward there. A $- \to +$ crossing makes $x = 3$ the minimum of $f$.
feedback.incorrect: This line is $f'$. The minimum of $f$ is where $f'$ passes through zero going from negative (f falling) to positive (f rising) — find where the line crosses the $x$-axis.
feedback.hint: Set $f'(x) = 2x - 6 = 0$, so $x = 3$. Left of it $f' < 0$ (f falling); right of it $f' > 0$ (f rising) — a minimum.

content.body: Below is the graph of the DERIVATIVE $f'(x) = -2x + 6$ of $f(x) = -x^2 + 6x$. Tap the $x$ where $f$ has its MAXIMUM.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point over this hill — the tangent slope thins to 0 right at the top, the spot where rising turns to falling.

feedback.correct: Yes — $f'(x) = -2x + 6$ is positive before $x = 3$ and negative after, crossing zero downward there. A $+ \to -$ crossing makes $x = 3$ the maximum of $f$.
feedback.incorrect: This line is $f'$. The maximum of $f$ is where $f'$ drops through zero from positive (f rising) to negative (f falling) — find where the falling line crosses the $x$-axis.
feedback.hint: Set $f'(x) = -2x + 6 = 0$, so $x = 3$. Left of it $f' > 0$ (f rising); right of it $f' < 0$ (f falling) — a maximum.

content.body: Put the steps for finding a function's maxima and minima in order.

items: Check the sign of $f'$ on each side
Classify each as max, min, or neither

sandbox.caption: Drag the point across this hill — feel the tangent slope go from positive, through 0 at the crest, to negative.

feedback.correct: Exactly — differentiate, solve f' = 0, test the sign on each side, then classify.
feedback.incorrect: You can't classify a point before you've found it. Start by differentiating.
feedback.hint: First get f', then where it's zero, then how its sign changes, then label each point.

content.body: For f(x) = x³ − 6x², the derivative is f'(x) = 3x² − 12x = 3x(x − 4), with critical points at x = 0 and x = 4. Label each interval.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point through this valley — the tangent slope is negative while the curve falls, 0 at the bottom, and positive as it rises.

feedback.correct: Right — 3x(x − 4) is positive for x < 0, negative on (0, 4), and positive for x > 4.
feedback.incorrect: Test a point in each interval in f'(x) = 3x(x − 4) and read its sign.
feedback.hint: At x = −1: +; at x = 2: −; at x = 5: +. So increasing, decreasing, increasing.

content.body: Match each slope-sign pattern to what it means for the curve.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point through this valley — watch the tangent slope pass through 0 and switch sign right at the turning point.

feedback.correct: Exactly — a sign flip in f' marks a turning point; no flip means no extremum.
feedback.incorrect: A max is rising then falling; a min is falling then rising; a steady sign means no turn.
feedback.hint: + → − tops out (max), − → + bottoms out (min), and a constant sign just keeps going.

content.body: Predict the maximum of f(x) = −x² + 4x: drag the dot to the peak, then lock it in to reveal the flat tangent.

interaction.goalLabel: Drag the dot to the maximum

sandbox.caption: Drag the point over this hill — the tangent slope eases to 0 exactly at the crest, then turns negative beyond it.

feedback.correct: Yes — the peak of −x² + 4x is at x = 2, where the tangent is flat (f'(2) = 0).
feedback.incorrect: The vertex sits halfway between the roots x = 0 and x = 4. Drag there and lock it in.
